tweets.title: Adani Defence & Aerospace has broken ground on India’s first integrated private-sector missile manufacturing facility in Shivpuri, Madhya Pradesh, with an investment of ₹2,500 crore. The facility will manufacture: 🔹 Missiles 🔹 Composite propellants 🔹 TNT and other critical energetic materials Designed to bridge the gap between DRDO-developed systems and mass production, the complex aims to accelerate induction of next-generation missile systems into service.
tweets.content:
Adani Defence & Aerospace has broken ground on India’s first integrated private-sector missile manufacturing facility in Shivpuri, Madhya Pradesh, with an investment of ₹2,500 crore.

The facility will manufacture:
🔹 Missiles
🔹 Composite propellants
🔹 TNT and other critical energetic materials

Designed to bridge the gap between DRDO-developed systems and mass production, the complex aims to accelerate induction of next-generation missile systems into service.

tweets.title: BANGLADESH APPROVES STARLINK TO EXPORT UNFILTERED INTERNET TO NEIGHBOURING COUNTRIES Bangladesh has approved Starlink to export unfiltered internet bandwidth to neighbouring countries through its telecom infrastructure. Unlike Bangladesh's domestic internet, which passes through government monitoring and filtering systems, the exported traffic will bypass those filters, providing direct international connectivity. Officials say: - The service is only for customers outside Bangladesh - Domestic internet traffic will remain separate and subject to existing regulations - Dedicated infrastructure and monitoring systems will keep domestic and export networks isolated The move marks Bangladesh's first approval for a satellite internet provider to export unfiltered international bandwidth, with Starlink sourcing capacity primarily from the state-owned Bangladesh Submarine Cable Company Limited (BSCCL).
tweets.content:
BANGLADESH APPROVES STARLINK TO EXPORT UNFILTERED INTERNET TO NEIGHBOURING COUNTRIES

Bangladesh has approved Starlink to export unfiltered internet bandwidth to neighbouring countries through its telecom infrastructure.

Unlike Bangladesh's domestic internet, which passes through government monitoring and filtering systems, the exported traffic will bypass those filters, providing direct international connectivity.

Officials say:
- The service is only for customers outside Bangladesh
- Domestic internet traffic will remain separate and subject to existing regulations
- Dedicated infrastructure and monitoring systems will keep domestic and export networks isolated

The move marks Bangladesh's first approval for a satellite internet provider to export unfiltered international bandwidth, with Starlink sourcing capacity primarily from the state-owned Bangladesh Submarine Cable Company Limited (BSCCL).

Key Features of INS Mahendragiri: - Stealth Technology: A special design that makes the ship hard to spot on enemy radar. - Heavy Firepower: Fitted with surface-to-air and surface-to-surface missiles to attack enemy ships, planes, and land targets. - Anti-Submarine Defense: Includes advanced systems to detect and fight underwater threats. - Design: Crafted by the Navy's own Warship Design Bureau. tweets.content:
INDIAN NAVY TO COMMISSION STEALTH FRIGATE INS MAHENDRAGIRI

The Indian Navy is set to commission INS Mahendragiri (F38), its sixth indigenous Project 17A stealth frigate, at Visakhapatnam on 11 July 2026.

Key Features of INS Mahendragiri:

- Stealth Technology: A special design that makes the ship hard to spot on enemy radar.
- Heavy Firepower: Fitted with surface-to-air and surface-to-surface missiles to attack enemy ships, planes, and land targets.
- Anti-Submarine Defense: Includes advanced systems to detect and fight underwater threats.
- Design: Crafted by the Navy's own Warship Design Bureau.

Named after the Mahendragiri mountain range in the Eastern Ghats, the warship was designed by the Indian Navy’s Warship Design Bureau (WDB) and built by Mazagon Dock Shipbuilders Limited (MDL) in Mumbai.

tweets.title: DIRCM is an important capability if an aircraft is expected to operate in environments where shoulder-fired MANPADS are a credible threat. It can also improve survivability against short-range IR-guided air-to-air missiles, although its effectiveness against modern imaging infrared (IIR) seekers is still not well understood in the public domain / attributable data. One design trade-off rarely discussed is RCS. Those large spherical DIRCM turrets are external protrusions, so it is worth asking whether they introduce a measurable radar cross-section penalty. The real question isn’t whether there is any increase, but whether the survivability gained against IR threats far outweighs any marginal increase in RCS. That’s an interesting engineering trade-off.
tweets.content:
DIRCM is an important capability if an aircraft is expected to operate in environments where shoulder-fired MANPADS are a credible threat. It can also improve survivability against short-range IR-guided air-to-air missiles, although its effectiveness against modern imaging infrared (IIR) seekers is still not well understood in the public domain / attributable data.

One design trade-off rarely discussed is RCS. Those large spherical DIRCM turrets are external protrusions, so it is worth asking whether they introduce a measurable radar cross-section penalty.

The real question isn’t whether there is any increase, but whether the survivability gained against IR threats far outweighs any marginal increase in RCS. That’s an interesting engineering trade-off.

tweets.title: I understand every argument in favour of Rafale, and I agree that India needs Rafale today, if not yesterday. But here’s the question I’m trying to answer. If the Government signs a deal for 94 Rafales to be manufactured in India, and deliveries begin in 2032, then even at a production rate of around 12 aircraft per year, the fleet would grow roughly like this: 2032: Deliveries begin 2033: 13 aircraft inducted 2034: 25 2035: 37 2036: 49 2037: 61 2038: 73 2039: 85 2040: 94 How does this solve the IAF’s immediate squadron shortage? The real question isn’t whether Rafale is an excellent aircraft.. it is. The question is: what is the bridge between now and 2032–2040? and this is F4 (not F5) P.S. I’ve deliberately simplified the induction timeline by assuming a near-steady production rate. In reality, there would likely be a production ramp-up in the initial years and a ramp-down towards the end, so the exact yearly numbers would differ. The point is to illustrate the overall timeline, not predict the precise delivery schedule.
tweets.content:
I understand every argument in favour of Rafale, and I agree that India needs Rafale today, if not yesterday.

But here’s the question I’m trying to answer.

If the Government signs a deal for 94 Rafales to be manufactured in India, and deliveries begin in 2032, then even at a production rate of around 12 aircraft per year, the fleet would grow roughly like this:

2032: Deliveries begin
2033: 13 aircraft inducted
2034: 25
2035: 37
2036: 49
2037: 61
2038: 73
2039: 85
2040: 94

How does this solve the IAF’s immediate squadron shortage?

The real question isn’t whether Rafale is an excellent aircraft.. it is. The question is: what is the bridge between now and 2032–2040? and this is F4 (not F5)

P.S. I’ve deliberately simplified the induction timeline by assuming a near-steady production rate. In reality, there would likely be a production ramp-up in the initial years and a ramp-down towards the end, so the exact yearly numbers would differ. The point is to illustrate the overall timeline, not predict the precise delivery schedule.
